title: DORA Major ICT Incident 7-Criteria Classification
exclusions: Classification refinement within the allowed assessment period; incidents at exact threshold boundaries requiring additional data collection; weekend/holiday incidents with next-business-day classification per procedures.
evidence: Major incident classification evidence showing all 7 Art.18 criteria assessments with data sources, threshold calculations, and classification decision records timestamped.

compliance.assurance_rationale: DORA Art.18 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that DORA Art.18 (Classification of ICT-related incidents) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.3.11: DORA Major ICT Incident 7-Criteria Classification.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.3.11' running on notable macro, index=itsi_summary, CIM Authentication and Network_Traffic data models, dora_service_client_mapping.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Submit a test incident for classification with incomplete data (missing client count and geographic spread fields); verify the classification timeliness search flags the incomplete classification with missing criteria identified.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Submit a test incident with all 7 Art.18 criteria fully assessed within 4 hours of detection; verify the classification shows as timely and complete.

dataSources: `` `notable` `` macro, `index=itsi_summary`, CIM Authentication and Network_Traffic data models, `dora_service_client_mapping.csv`
spl:
`notable` urgency IN ("high","critical") status!="Closed" earliest=-48h
| eval affected_systems=mvappend(src, dest)
| mvexpand affected_systems
| lookup dora_service_client_mapping.csv system_name AS affected_systems OUTPUT service_name, client_count, client_pct, countries_served, data_classification
| stats dc(affected_systems) as systems_hit, max(client_count) as max_clients_affected, max(client_pct) as max_client_pct,
        dc(countries_served) as geo_spread, values(data_classification) as data_types by rule_name, urgency
| eval c1_clients=if(max_client_pct > 10 OR max_clients_affected > 500, 1, 0)
| eval c2_geographic=if(geo_spread > 1, 1, 0)
| eval c3_duration="ASSESS_MANUALLY"
| eval c4_data_loss=if(match(mvjoin(data_types,","),"(?i)confidential|restricted|pii|financial"), 1, 0)
| eval criteria_met=c1_clients + c2_geographic + c4_data_loss
| eval classification=if(criteria_met >= 2, "MAJOR — mandatory reporting", "Significant or below — assess remaining criteria")
| eval reporting_deadline=if(classification="MAJOR — mandatory reporting", "4h initial + 72h intermediate + 1mo final", "Monitor")
| table rule_name, urgency, systems_hit, max_clients_affected, max_client_pct, geo_spread, data_types, criteria_met, classification, reporting_deadline
| sort - criteria_met
description: DORA Article 18 and RTS 2024/1772 define seven classification criteria for determining whether an ICT incident is "major" — an incident meeting two or more criteria thresholds triggers mandatory reporting. This use case automates the classification assessment against all seven criteria, replacing manual spreadsheet-based classification and accelerating the 4-hour initial notification deadline.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for DORA compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsi_summary. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Build `dora_service_client_mapping.csv` mapping ICT systems to business services, client counts/percentages, geographic reach, and data classifications; (2) automate criteria 1 (clients), 2 (geographic), and 4 (data loss) — criteria 3 (duration), 5-7 require manual assessment initially; (3) alert immediately when criteria_met >= 2 — the 4-hour reporting clock starts; (4) integrate with SOAR for automated notification workflow; (5) document classification rationale for each incident. Indexes required: `index=itsi_summary`.
visualization: Table (incident classification results), Single value (major incidents requiring reporting), Bar chart (criteria met per incident), Traffic light (classification status).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Classification criteria refinement** — Initial classification using preliminary information may require revision as incident scope becomes clearer; interim classifications are not final.

2. **Threshold boundary incidents** — Incidents falling exactly at classification boundaries (e.g., affecting exactly 10% of clients) require human judgment that may take time to resolve.

3. **Data availability for classification** — Some Art.18 criteria (economic impact, geographic spread) may not be immediately available, causing delayed but accurate classification.

4. **Interconnected service impact assessment** — Determining whether service disruption affects critical or important functions requires BIA validation that adds classification time.

5. **Weekend/holiday classification delays** — Incidents discovered outside business hours may have classification completed at the start of the next business day per the escalation procedures.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`dora_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly per the ICT risk management framework review cycle.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Lookups**: `dora_service_client_mapping.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('dora_service_client_mapping.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.18`. The control objective is: "Evidence that DORA Art.18 (Classification of ICT-related incidents) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.3.11: DORA Major ICT Incident 7-Criteria Classification." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Build `dora_service_client_mapping.csv` mapping ICT systems to business services, client counts/percentages, geographic reach, and data classifications. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 2:** automate criteria 1 (clients), 2 (geographic), and 4 (data loss) — criteria 3 (duration), 5-7 require manual assessment initially. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** alert immediately when criteria_met >= 2 — the 4-hour reporting clock starts. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 4:** integrate with SOAR for automated notification workflow. Automated remediation must be gated behind a defined approval workflow; compliance controls that take destructive action without human review are themselves a governance risk.

• **Hint 5:** document classification rationale for each incident. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "DORA Major ICT Incident 7-Criteria Classification," the aggregation summarises incident flow — volume per category, time-to-contain distribution, and resolution cadence are the metrics regulators ask about during post-incident review.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `dora_service_client_mapping.csv` — the enrichment table that adds compliance-relevant business context to raw events. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`service_name, client_count, client_pct, countries_served, data_classification`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `system_name`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `dora_service_client_mapping.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval system_name = lower(system_name)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 48 hours.

Numeric threshold `max_client_pct > 10`: this is the noise-to-signal decision point for the detection. Calibrate against your estate's baseline — see the 'Troubleshooting' section below for the calibration query.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.3.11: DORA Major ICT Incident 7-Criteria Classification]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -48h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.3.11,reg=DORA
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `affected_systems`, `c1_clients`, `c2_geographic`, `c3_duration`, `c4_data_loss`, `classification`, `criteria_met`, `data_types`, `geo_spread`, `max_client_pct`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table affected_systems, c1_clients, c2_geographic, c3_duration, c4_data_loss, classification, criteria_met, data_types
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `dora_service_client_mapping.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup dora_service_client_mapping.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `max_client_pct`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(max_client_pct) as p50 perc90(max_client_pct) as p90 perc99(max_client_pct) as p99 max(max_client_pct) as hi
```

The current threshold `10` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.3.11: DORA evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`incident classification results`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Single-value KPI (`major incidents requiring reporting`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'DORA current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 3 — Distribution (`criteria met per incident`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.

**Panel 4 — Traffic Light (`classification status`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**DORA-specific failure modes:**

• Third-party ICT risk register (Art. 28): DORA requires a Register of Information listing every critical ICT third-party service provider. Detections touching supplier risk must reference this register; if a supplier appears in telemetry but not in the Register, that is a Register-completeness finding, not just a technical anomaly.

• Testing cadence (Art. 24-27): DORA's TLPT (Threat-Led Penetration Testing) is required every 3 years for significant institutions. Detections measuring resilience testing must differentiate TLPT (supervisor-led) from self-assessment — mixing them dilutes the evidence.

• Incident classification thresholds (Art. 18): DORA uses specific criteria for 'major' vs 'significant' incidents — client impact, duration, data volume. Detections must classify against these specific thresholds, which differ from GDPR breach criteria; a single event can be major-DORA but not a personal-data breach.

• Resilience testing record retention (Art. 27): results of digital operational resilience tests must be retained. Align log retention to match — typically 5+ years. Verify `frozenTimePeriodInSecs >= 157680000` on any index named by a DORA resilience-testing UC.
grandmaExplanation: We dORA Article 18 and RTS 2024/1772 define seven classification criteria for determining whether an ICT incident is "major" — an incident meeting two or more criteria thresholds triggers mandatory reporting.
